Precedent Study Datasheet

Overview

Project Overview & Metadata
Forensic Architecture
Project Overview

This project traces a century of border design in the Evros / Meriç River region, revealing how infrastructures, policies, and technologies shape the production of borders and human movement.

It combines investigative research, spatial analysis, and open-source techniques to expose state violence and advocate for human rights.

▱Combining architecture, law, journalism, and the sciences.
◉Using open-source investigations and spatial technologies.
♙Working with multidisciplinary teams and collaborators.

Author Bibliography

Studio Profile · Team · Author Bibliography
About Forensic Architecture

Forensic Architecture is a multidisciplinary research agency based at Goldsmiths, University of London.

It combines architecture, media analysis, and open-source intelligence to investigate human rights violations.

Eyal WeizmanFounder & Director
Sofia PantouvakiHead of Investigations

Ontological Analysis

Components · Systems · Border Apparatus
Border Apparatus

The project shows that the Evros/Meriç border is not a single line. It is a socio-technical apparatus produced by river movement, legal documents, military infrastructure, surveillance systems, migration routes, and evidence platforms.

Its ontology is therefore made of relationships, not only objects.

≈
Natural SystemRiverIslandsSandbanksWetlandsErosionWildfire
§
Legal SystemTreaty of LausanneAthens Protocol1926 BoundaryAsylum Law
▥
InfrastructureFenceWatchtowerPatrol RoadCanalLeveeAntenna
♙
Human ActorsAsylum SeekersBorder GuardsNGOsResearchersGovernments
▧
EvidenceArchive MapsSatellite ImagesGPSVideosTestimonyGIS
◎
Digital PlatformInteractive MapTimelineDatabaseCoordinate ToolArchive Viewer
Critical Point: The project demonstrates that the border is not a fixed geopolitical line but an assemblage continuously produced through environment, law, infrastructure, surveillance, evidence, and humanitarian practice.

PART II
04Ontological Analysis05History & Context06Visual & Aesthetic Representation
Evros / Meriç River
Forensic Architecture
2025

History & Context

Historical Background · Context · Critical Response
1923Treaty of Lausanne defines the river as border.
1926Athens Protocol fixes the legal boundary.
1926–2024River shifts over time.
9,404 haLegal border and current river form a territorial grey zone.
2020–2024Pushbacks and stranded groups intensify.
2023Wildfire exposes environmental risk and border violence.
2025Forensic platform maps evidence and accountability.
Historical Background

The Evros/Meriç River officially became the international border between Greece and Turkey after the Treaty of Lausanne in 1923. Between 1923 and 1926, an international Boundary Commission surveyed the river and produced a precise legal demarcation, later documented in the Athens Protocol.

Rather than allowing the border to follow future river changes, the commission fixed the boundary according to the river's position in January 1926. This transformed a dynamic landscape into a supposedly stable geopolitical line.

Border Transformation

Over the next century, flooding, erosion, sediment deposition, and hydraulic engineering altered the river's course. As the landscape changed, the legal boundary remained frozen in its 1926 position.

This produced approximately 9,404 hectares of territorial ambiguity, where the legal border and the present-day river no longer coincide. The key question becomes less “who owns the land?” and more “who is responsible for people trapped there?”

Political & Humanitarian Context

Since the mid-2010s, the river has become a major migration route into the European Union. Many asylum seekers fleeing conflict, persecution, or instability travel through Turkey before attempting to enter Greece.

At the same time, fences, patrol roads, surveillance towers, thermal cameras, drones, and military buffer zones have transformed the river into a militarised border environment.

Project Response

Forensic Architecture treats the border not as a fixed line but as a constructed border apparatus. The project combines archive maps, georeferenced cartography, satellite imagery, GIS analysis, GPS coordinates, testimony, OSINT, and spatial reconstruction.

Through this method, mapping becomes forensic evidence. The platform reveals how law, landscape, engineering, surveillance, and political decision-making collectively produce contemporary border violence.

Critical Point: The platform links historical border design to contemporary humanitarian crisis, showing how a century-old legal line still shapes present-day violence.
◎

Visual & Aesthetic Representation

Visual Language · Representation · Evidence
Visual Chain
01Archive Maps
02Historical Documents
03Georeferencing
04Satellite Imagery
05GIS Layers
06Interactive Platform
Visual Language

Unlike conventional architectural drawings, the project adopts the visual language of forensic investigation. Archive maps, satellite imagery, GIS layers, testimony, and spatial reconstruction work together to transform mapping into evidence rather than illustration.

Constructing the Argument

The platform builds its argument through comparison. Historical maps are georeferenced and overlaid with present-day satellite imagery, while additional layers reveal stranded groups, infrastructure, river migration, and fire progression.

Archive Aesthetic

Paper textures, historical cartography, muted colours, red boundary overlays, documentary typography, and layered GIS visualisations encourage close reading and reinforce the credibility of the investigation.

Representational Community

The project belongs to Forensic Architecture’s interdisciplinary field, drawing from critical cartography, GIS, OSINT, investigative journalism, human-rights documentation, and environmental humanities.

Critical Reflection

Rather than illustrating a political issue, the platform transforms architectural representation into evidence. Through mapping, georeferencing, and spatial reconstruction, it exposes hidden systems of border violence and supports humanitarian accountability.

Prepared by
